about.title: Making Deep-Tech Real, One Improbable Bet at a Time

intro.title: Making Deep-Tech Real, One Improbable Bet at a Time
columns: I've got about 10 years of staying in the game. building early-stage startups. making things that are worth making. stanford gsb, 2025. est. 2015.
Mildly opinionated about most things. moderately opinionated about Spatial Computing. deeply opinionated about dosas. founded 15 ventures. one exit to walmart.
I build and invest in deep-tech. the wild bets. the blurry line between improbable and impossible. not just writing cheques - zero to one, hands in the code.

whatWeBuild.title: What We Build
paragraphs: We build storytelling platforms that demand attention and reward curiosity. We push digital mediums to places you haven't seen before, and have fun doing it. Beyond code, we offer 3D design and animation, UI and motion design, concepts and digital strategy, full-stack development, and creative consulting.

Whether it's prototyping an idea, launching an augmented reality experience, or bringing high-fidelity visuals to life, Shader bridges the gap between creative ambition and technical execution. Our process is hands-on, collaborative, and tailored for teams that value both craft and innovation. We combine technical expertise with a designer's eye, ensuring that every interaction feels natural and every pixel is perfectly placed. We're not your regular IT department. We don't troubleshoot printers.

clients.title: Built and Backed
clients.paragraph: I've been a tinkerer all through. founded, funded, and figured out life in 15 early-stage startups. exited Scapic to Walmart | Flipkart. backed by Binny Bansal.

serious.title: For Founders Serious About the Future
serious.paragraph:
Things that are worth making shape the future in positive ways. but getting these things out of the lab and into people's lives isn't easy.

I build and invest in deep-tech. the wild bets. founded 15 ventures. exited one to walmart. not just writing cheques, zero to one, hands in the code.

Stanford gsb 2025. 10 years in the game. Submit a pitch. I invest in things and friends I know.

antiPortfolio.title: Anti-Portfolio
antiPortfolio.subline: For a few things that work, many don't. My list of attempts that went to the graveyard. Failure is part of the process.

items.name: Vagabond
items.oneLiner: A roaming social app for people who lived out of a backpack. Turns out nomads don't want another app, they want better wifi.

items.name: Beep Collective
items.oneLiner: Audio-first social network. We made noise. The market made silence.

items.name: Grail
items.oneLiner: A search engine for collectibles. Found a lot of things. Not a business model.

items.name: Culture Tools
items.oneLiner: SaaS for shaping company culture. Companies preferred to keep shaping it with free Notion pages.

items.name: Nada
items.oneLiner: An AI platform that analysed a track and then isolated each stem to let you download the same. Worked great. The business model? Not so much.

items.name: Pixel
items.oneLiner: Home automation built around inexpensive switching systems and a hub called Rally. Beautiful hardware. Wrong decade.

items.name: Augmented Shirts
items.oneLiner: AR + physical T-shirts that triggered messages, animations, and more when you pointed your phone. Compelling idea. Needed more marketing than tech.

items.name: RCB Cards
items.oneLiner: 9,999 NFT cricket cards with AR effects and utilities. DOA thanks to cricket licensing complications.

items.name: Kooriee
items.oneLiner: Hyperlocal logistics for long-tail brands. Worked in one city. Unit economics refused to leave it.

items.name: Specto
items.oneLiner: Cardboard-era VR cases for mobile phones. The industry moved on to standalone before we could.

items.name: User Stories Comics
items.oneLiner: A comic series about making accessible VR. Fun to make. Webcomic economics ate it alive.

items.name: Micro Dark Wares
items.oneLiner: Dark stores in Bangalore promising 10-minute delivery. Pandemic gave it life, normalcy took it back.
